items.question: What is the difference between an iterable and an iterator?
items.answer: An iterable is anything you can loop over; calling iter() on it returns an iterator. An iterator is the stateful object that actually produces values via next() and raises StopIteration when exhausted. Lists are iterable; the object iter(list) returns is an iterator.

items.question: How does a generator differ from a regular function?
items.answer: A regular function runs to completion and returns once. A generator function contains yield, so calling it returns a generator object; each next() runs until the next yield, pausing and preserving local state. It produces a sequence lazily instead of all at once.

items.question: Why are generators memory-efficient?
items.answer: They compute and hand back one value at a time and don't store the whole sequence, so memory stays roughly constant regardless of how many items are produced — essential for large files, streams or infinite sequences.

items.question: What happens if you iterate a generator twice?
items.answer: Nothing the second time — generators are single-pass. Once exhausted they yield no more values. If you need to iterate again, recreate the generator or materialize it into a list first (which uses memory).
